metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the explicit `TypedDict` / dataclass schema for `audit_trail`'s return value to the plan (field names `plan_reviews`, `completion`, `code_reviews` or equivalent, plus nullability rules when a record is absent), so the integration test oracle and MCP tool schema can be written unambiguously.
findings.finding: The 'typed dict' returned by `audit_trail` has no specified field schema — field names, nesting structure, and nullability for missing records (e.g. no completion sidecar) are all left to executor discretion, which means the integration test assertion and the MCP tool response shape cannot be written deterministically from the plan alone.
findings.suggested_fix:
Add a schema block to the Scope section, e.g.:
```python
class AuditTrail(TypedDict):
    plan_reviews: list[dict]   # newest-first; empty list if none
    completion: dict | None    # None if no completion record
    code_reviews: list[dict]   # each with its own sidecar history; empty list if no relates_to links
```
and update AC1's oracle to assert key names explicitly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py` (intercept arm for `audit`) and `src/rebar/_cli/help/audit.txt` (help text) to the Scope's list of files to edit.
findings.finding: The plan names `rebar audit show <ticket> [--output json]` as a new CLI command but does not enumerate the required edit targets for CLI wiring: (1) an intercept arm in `src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py` (all existing custom subcommands like `review`, `review-code`, `workflow`, `enrich`, etc. each have an explicit `if argv and argv[0] == '<subcmd>':` intercept there), and (2) a help file `src/rebar/_cli/help/audit.txt` (required for `known_subcommands()` to recognize `audit` and for `rebar audit --help` to work).
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Scope: '- `src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py` — add intercept `if argv and argv[0] == "audit":` routing to the new audit CLI handler, following the pattern of `review`, `enrich`, etc.' and '- `src/rebar/_cli/help/audit.txt` — help text for `rebar audit show`'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/_mcp_reads.py` (register the `audit_trail` read-only tool) to the Scope's list of files to edit.
findings.finding: The plan names a new MCP `audit_trail` tool but does not enumerate the required edit targets for MCP registration: the tool must be registered via one of the registrar modules (`_mcp_reads.py` is the natural home for a read-only tool) and `mcp_server.py`'s `build_server()` already calls `register_read_tools(mcp, ctx)` — so `_mcp_reads.py` needs editing. Neither `_mcp_reads.py` nor `mcp_server.py` appears in the plan's Scope.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Scope: '- `src/rebar/_mcp_reads.py` — register `audit_trail` as a `READ_ONLY`-annotated tool using `@mcp.tool(annotations=_ANN["READ_ONLY"])`, following the pattern of `show_ticket` / `list_tickets`'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the typed dict structure for `audit_trail`'s return value: specify at minimum the top-level keys (e.g. `plan_reviews`, `completion`, `code_reviews`) and the type of each value so AC-1/AC-2/AC-3 oracles can be written against a stable contract.
findings.finding: The plan specifies that audit_trail returns 'a typed dict' but never defines its keys, field names, types, or nesting structure. This is a schema-referenced-but-vocabulary-undefined gap: the return type is the primary contract of the new library function, yet an implementer has no field specification to code against or test against.
findings.location: Scope section: 'returning a typed dict'; AC-1: 'audit_trail(ticket_id) returns the full retained plan-review history, the completion record, and the code reviews linked via relates_to, newest-first'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Return schema' sub-section to Scope or AC-1, e.g.: `{"ticket_id": str, "plan_reviews": list[dict], "completion": {"attestation": dict|None, "sidecar": dict|None}, "code_reviews": list[{"ticket_id": str, "sidecars": list[dict]}]}`. A TypedDict in `read.py` serves the same purpose at implementation time.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add ACs (or extend AC1) with test oracles for: (a) `audit_trail` on a ticket with no `relates_to` links returns an empty code-reviews list rather than raising; (b) `audit_trail` on a non-existent ticket_id returns a defined empty/error structure; (c) a sidecar seeded as a v1 record is correctly schema-coerced and appears in the history (validating the claimed schema-tolerance).
findings.finding: The plan specifies no test oracle for failure/empty/invalid paths of the new user-facing `audit_trail` function and its CLI/MCP exposures: (a) ticket_id that does not exist, (b) ticket with zero `relates_to` links (no linked code reviews — the common real-world case), and (c) a sidecar record that is malformed/schema-v1 when the plan explicitly claims schema-tolerance as a delivered property.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria (AC1, AC2, AC3) and Scope section ('schema-tolerant readers accept both v1 and v2 records')
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC1: 'Additionally: (a) seed a ticket with no relates_to links and assert `code_reviews` is an empty list; (b) call `audit_trail` on a nonexistent id and assert the return value matches the documented absent-ticket contract (e.g. raises TicketNotFound or returns None with defined shape); (c) seed one v1-format plan-review sidecar alongside a v2 one and assert both appear in the history.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the link-traversal description: the work ticket's code-review links are reachable via its outgoing `deps` list (filtered to `relation=='relates_to'`), not via `inbound_links` — specify that `audit_trail` should read `show_ticket(ticket_id)['deps']`, filter to `relation=='relates_to'`, resolve each target's `ticket_type`, and keep those that are `code_review`.
findings.finding: The plan's implementation description for resolving code-review links is technically incorrect in a way that would cause an implementer to traverse the wrong data field and find zero results. The plan states 'resolving code reviews via the ticket's inbound relates_to links from code_review tickets.' In reality, emit_code_review_artifact calls rebar.link(artifact_id, work_ticket_id, 'relates_to') (voter.py:278) — the code_review artifact is the link SOURCE. Because relates_to is symmetric, graph/_links.py:264-267 also writes a reciprocal event in the work ticket's directory, giving the work ticket an OUTGOING dep to the artifact. But reducer/_inbound.py:114 explicitly suppresses inbound_links entries for symmetric relations when the subject already has that outgoing dep (own_symmetric check). An implementer reading the plan who calls find_inbound_relationships() or inspects show_ticket()['inbound_links'] for code_review entries will find nothing; the correct traversal is via the work ticket's outgoing deps (show_ticket(work_ticket_id)['deps']) filtered to relation=='relates_to', then resolving ticket_type=='code_review'.
findings.location: Approach rationale: 'resolving code reviews via the ticket's inbound relates_to links from code_review tickets'; Scope section: 'resolving code reviews via the ticket's relates_to links (reusing rebar.show / rebar.list_tickets)'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope and Approach rationale sections, replace 'resolving code reviews via the ticket's inbound relates_to links from code_review tickets' with: 'resolving code reviews via the work ticket's outgoing deps list (show_ticket(ticket_id)["deps"]) filtered to relation=="relates_to", then selecting entries whose resolved ticket has ticket_type=="code_review" — the symmetric write in graph/_links.py ensures the work ticket carries an outgoing dep to the artifact, but reducer/_inbound.py suppresses the inbound_links entry for symmetric relations when the outgoing dep already exists.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the link-traversal description: code_review artifacts link *to* the work ticket (code_review is the source); the work ticket sees the code_review as an **outgoing** `relates_to` dep (via symmetric reciprocal write in `_links.py`), not as an inbound_link — implement `audit_trail` by reading `show_ticket(work_ticket_id)['deps']` filtered to `relation=='relates_to'` and `ticket_type=='code_review'`, not by reading `inbound_links`.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that audit_trail resolves code reviews 'via the ticket's inbound relates_to links from code_review tickets', but the actual link topology is the opposite: emit_code_review_artifact calls rebar.link(artifact_id, work_ticket_id, 'relates_to'), making the code_review artifact the link source. Because relates_to is symmetric (_SYMMETRIC_RELATIONS in reducer/_inbound.py), a reciprocal event IS written in the work ticket's directory, so the work ticket's compiled state shows the code_review as an OUTGOING dep — NOT as an inbound_link (which _inbound.py:114 actively suppresses for symmetric relations when the outgoing dep already exists). The plan's implementation note ('resolving code reviews via the ticket's inbound relates_to links') would lead an implementer to read inbound_links from show_ticket output, but those entries are suppressed; the correct traversal is via the outgoing deps list filtered to relation=='relates_to' and ticket_type=='code_review'.
findings.location: Approach rationale section: 'resolving code reviews via the ticket's inbound relates_to links from code_review tickets'; also Scope section: 'resolving code reviews via the ticket's relates_to links'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope/Approach sections, replace 'inbound relates_to links from code_review tickets' with 'outgoing relates_to deps whose target is a code_review ticket (written via the symmetric reciprocal by graph/_links.py)'. In read.py, implement: `[d for d in show_ticket(ticket_id).get('deps', []) if d.get('relation') == 'relates_to' and show_ticket(d['target_id']).get('ticket_type') == 'code_review']`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the default output format for `rebar audit show <ticket>` (without `--output json`): either declare JSON as the default, describe a human-readable summary format, or remove the optional flag and always emit JSON.
findings.finding: The default output format of `rebar audit show <ticket>` (without `--output json`) is unspecified — the plan only tests the `--output json` path, leaving the human-readable default undefined.
findings.location: Scope section — 'CLI `rebar audit show <ticket> [--output json]`'; AC2
findings.suggested_fix: Simplest resolution: make JSON the default (and only) output format, dropping the optional flag, which also simplifies the AC oracle to a single path.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the 'v2 tolerance' protocol precisely: either enumerate the accepted schema strings (e.g. `plan_review_result_v1`, `plan_review_result_v2`) or specify a schema-prefix/fallback rule so the `all_review_results` enumerators have a concrete guard to implement.
findings.finding: The plan states 'Schema-tolerant readers accept both v1 and v2 records' but no v2 schema vocabulary exists in the codebase (confirmed: only plan_review_result_v1, code_review_result_v1, completion_verifier_fail_v1 are defined), and the plan never defines what a v2 record looks like. An implementer writing the schema-tolerance guard has no specification to code against.
findings.location: Compatibility & rollback section: 'Schema-tolerant readers accept both v1 and v2 records.'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'accept both v1 and v2 records' with a concrete rule, e.g.: 'schema-tolerant means: accept any payload whose `schema` field starts with the expected prefix (e.g. `plan_review_result_`) or matches a listed set; skip payloads with an unrecognized schema and log a warning.' Until a v2 schema exists, stating 'accept v1; skip unknown schemas with a warning' is sufficient and honest.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the plan's reference to 'reusing `rebar.show` / `rebar.list_tickets`' for inbound link traversal with the correct API (`rebar.find_inbound_relationships(ticket_id, tracker_dir)`) and add it to the Scope's named symbols.
findings.finding: The plan says `audit_trail` resolves code-review tickets 'via the ticket's inbound `relates_to` links' by 'reusing `rebar.show` / `rebar.list_tickets`', but neither `rebar.show_ticket` nor `rebar.list_tickets` returns inbound links. The `show_state` implementation explicitly notes it 'never augmented with inbound_links / children.' The correct symbol is `rebar.find_inbound_relationships()`, which exists and is re-exported — but is not named in the plan's scope or approach.
findings.location: Scope section / Approach rationale — 'resolving code reviews via the ticket's inbound `relates_to` links (reusing `rebar.show` / `rebar.list_tickets`)'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope section, replace 'reusing `rebar.show` / `rebar.list_tickets`' with 'reusing `rebar.find_inbound_relationships` to walk inbound `relates_to` links, then `rebar.show_ticket` to fetch each resolved code-review ticket.' Note the O(corpus-scan) cost in the approach rationale.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/audit/__init__.py` to the Scope's list of new files.
findings.finding: The plan creates `src/rebar/audit/read.py` but does not mention `src/rebar/audit/__init__.py`. Python requires an `__init__.py` for a package directory to be importable as `rebar.audit`; without it, `from rebar.audit.read import audit_trail` will fail unless implicit namespace packages are relied upon (which the existing codebase does not use — every package directory has an `__init__.py`).
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Scope: '- `src/rebar/audit/__init__.py` — empty (or minimal docstring) package init, following the pattern of `src/rebar/attest/__init__.py`'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `audit_trail` resolves linked `code_review` tickets by reading the subject ticket's own `deps` (filtering for `relation=="relates_to"`) and resolving each target's `ticket_type`, rather than scanning all `code_review` tickets via `list_tickets` — the symmetric `relates_to` link already places the artifact IDs in the work ticket's outgoing deps.
findings.finding: The plan names `rebar.list_tickets` as the primitive for resolving which `code_review` tickets are linked to a work ticket, but `relates_to` is a symmetric (bidirectional) link — the work ticket's own compiled `deps` (returned by `rebar.show_ticket`) already contains the exact `code_review` artifact IDs as outgoing `relates_to` entries. Using `list_tickets(ticket_type='code_review')` is an O(total code_review tickets) corpus scan, whereas filtering the subject ticket's `deps` for `relation=="relates_to"` and verifying each target's `ticket_type` is an O(linked-count) direct lookup. The existing `latest_code_review_result` in `code_review/sidecar.py` uses the expensive list_tickets pattern only because it must match by title rather than by ID — here the IDs are already present in `deps`, so that cost is unnecessary.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope / Approach rationale, replace the `rebar.list_tickets` reference for code-review resolution with: 'reading the subject ticket's `deps` via `rebar.show_ticket(ticket_id)["deps"]`, filtering for `relation=="relates_to"` entries whose resolved ticket has `ticket_type=="code_review"`' — this reuses the symmetric link the reviewbot already writes at O(linked-count) instead of O(all code_review tickets).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct 'rebar.show' to 'rebar.show_ticket' and consider noting find_inbound_relationships (rebar.reducer.find_inbound_relationships) as the in-process primitive for inbound relates_to traversal.
findings.finding: The plan cites 'reusing rebar.show / rebar.list_tickets' as the primitives for resolving code-review tickets via relates_to links, but rebar.show does not exist in the public API — the correct function is rebar.show_ticket (confirmed at rebar/__init__.py:77). This is a referenced-but-nonexistent symbol.
findings.location: Scope section: 'resolving code reviews via the ticket's relates_to links (reusing rebar.show / rebar.list_tickets)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'reusing rebar.show / rebar.list_tickets' with 'reusing rebar.show_ticket / rebar.find_inbound_relationships (for inbound relates_to traversal) / rebar.list_tickets'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion (or extend AC1) that directly verifies `all_review_results(ticket)` on both sidecar modules: correct API name, newest-first ordering across multiple seeded events, and schema-tolerance across both v1 and v2 records.
findings.finding: The `all_review_results(ticket)` enumerators added to both sidecar modules are named as explicit, distinct deliverables in Scope but have no direct covering acceptance criterion; they are only exercised implicitly through AC1's audit_trail integration test, meaning their public API shape (name, signature, schema-tolerant ordering) is not independently verified.
findings.location: Scope section (`src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py` and `src/rebar/llm/code_review/sidecar.py` bullets) vs Acceptance Criteria (AC1)
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC1 or add AC5: 'Oracle: unit tests on `plan_review.sidecar.all_review_results` and `code_review.sidecar.all_review_results` assert: (a) results are ordered newest→oldest; (b) both v1 and v2 sidecar records are returned without error; (c) an empty history returns an empty sequence.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Capture the audit-layer architectural decision (single aggregation surface over plan/code-review sidecars via relates_to links) in CLAUDE.md or a lightweight ADR so future contributors know where to extend it.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new cross-subsystem audit aggregation layer (audit/read.py coupling to both rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py and rebar/llm/code_review/sidecar.py) without capturing this architectural decision in an ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design doc.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a CLAUDE.md entry or docs/adr/audit-read-layer.md noting: audit/read.py is the canonical read surface for all review histories; new review types should add an all_review_results() enumerator in their sidecar module and register it in audit_trail() — not bypass the layer.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define and implement the error contract for `audit_trail()`: specify what it returns/raises for ticket-not-found, partial `relates_to` resolution failures, and unreadable sidecars; add at least one AC covering an error/partial-data scenario; and add structured logging at each failure point in the read path.
findings.finding: The plan specifies no error-handling behavior for the `audit_trail()` aggregator or its CLI/MCP consumers when underlying reads fail (e.g., ticket ID not found, missing or unreadable sidecar files, `rebar.show`/`rebar.list_tickets` raising), and no observability (logging/tracing) is specified for the new read surface's failure points.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the plan: (1) `audit_trail()` raises `TicketNotFoundError` for unknown ticket IDs and returns partial results (with a `warnings` list) when individual sidecar reads fail, logging each skipped record at WARNING level; (2) add an AC: 'When a linked code_review ticket has no sidecar, `audit_trail` returns the ticket reference with an empty history and a warning entry rather than failing.'; (3) note that the CLI prints warnings to stderr and exits 0 on partial results, non-zero on total failure.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add non-happy-path specs to the CLI and MCP ACs: define behavior (error message + non-zero exit code) for an invalid/missing ticket ID, and define the return shape when any of the three history buckets (plan-review, completion, code-reviews) is empty or absent.
findings.finding: The CLI and MCP tool specs cover only the happy path; non-happy-path states — invalid/missing ticket ID, ticket with no plan-review sidecars, missing completion record, no `relates_to` links (partial or fully empty audit trail) — are not addressed anywhere in the plan, and no error-exit-code or error-message behavior is specified.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2 (`rebar audit show`) and AC3 (MCP tool); Scope section CLI/MCP description
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC2 to include a second oracle: a subprocess test asserting a non-zero exit code and stderr message when the ticket does not exist. Extend AC1 (and by extension AC3) to state that missing buckets are represented as empty lists/null fields (not omitted), so callers have a stable shape regardless of data completeness.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
